rules.description: Consumer Rights Act statutory information
Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees
Subscription traders must send reminders before renewal and offer one-click cancellation.
Customer-facing websites must offer reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010.
Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page
Contact details of the controller (including DPO if applicable)
Purposes of processing AND legal basis
International transfers and safeguards (SCCs / adequacy)
Retention period or criteria
Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object
Right to withdraw consent (if consent is the basis)
Right to complain to ICO
Analytics cookies require consent (ICO position 2023)
Cookie banner must not use pre-ticked boxes
Cookie wall (pay-or-OK) for unrelated content is not freely-given consent
Soft opt-in: prior similar product/service customer relationship for marketing
Marketing emails must include sender identity (no false header)
Marketing emails must include valid return address
Easy opt-out mechanism in every marketing email
Cookie consent before non-essential cookies set
Cookie reject must be as prominent as accept
Cookie purpose disclosed clearly before consent
Marketing must not be misleading or unfair
Specialist or expert claims must be substantiated
Solicitors must publish complaints procedure including LeO route.
Solicitors must publish price information for personal injury work.
Solicitors must display SRA regulation statement + firm number.
Price information accessible on all relevant practice areas
Complaints procedure published online
Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)
Author byline on substantive content (Experience + Expertise)
Author credentials visible (Authoritativeness)
Valid consent and an easy withdrawal route
Prior consent for marketing communications
Privacy notice: identity, purpose, recipients, risks, rights
Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews)
All-in pricing, no hidden mandatory fees (DMCC drip-pricing ban)
UK limited companies must display registered name, number, registered office on every website.
Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires clear rights notice on commercial websites.
Total price including mandatory fees must be displayed up-front, not added at checkout.
Must publish a policy declaring no incentivised, fake or undisclosed reviews.
Reviews or testimonials shown without disclosing incentives or a genuine-review verification process
Clear subscription terms + easy cancellation (DMCC)
Section 3 personal data definition disclosure
Legitimate interests (if relied on) clearly stated
Recipients or categories of recipients of personal data
Whether provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of not providing
Existence of automated decision-making including profiling
Cookie purposes must be itemised (functional vs analytics vs marketing)
Cookie list must include third-party cookies
Cookie banner copy must be available before clicking accept
Cookie consent log must be retained for 12 months minimum
Re-prompt for consent after material changes to cookie usage
Commercial organisations turnover >£36M must publish annual Modern Slavery Act statement.
Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them before consent
Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
B2B corporate subscriber: soft opt-in still required for individual employees identifiable in role
Cookie withdrawal mechanism accessible from every page
Firms must publish annual diversity statistics under the SRA Code.
SRA-regulated firm digital badge displayed
Solicitor name and SRA number on website footer
Complaints procedure + Legal Ombudsman + SRA routes
Publish prices for the listed services (SRA Transparency Rules)
Unit pricing for pre-packaged goods
Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)
Published content shows author / expertise signals (E-E-A-T)
Data subject rights (access, copy, correction, deletion, withdraw)
Cross-border transfer conditions disclosed (Saudi PDPL)
Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
VAT-registered businesses must show VAT number on website per HMRC guidance.
Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
Fair, transparent and legible consumer contract terms
Age-appropriate design (ICO Children Code)
Section 22 manual unstructured data exemption noted where applicable
Image alt text as a reasonable adjustment
UK right of access (Subject Access Request)
UK right to erasure
UK right to data portability
UK right to object to direct marketing
UK processor / sub-processor transparency
UK records of processing (ROPA)
UK lawful basis identified
Cookie scan must distinguish session vs persistent cookies
Cookie banner not shown to authenticated users with stored preferences
Valid marketing consent or soft opt-in for email/SMS
Strictly necessary cookies do not require consent but must be disclosed
Regulatory information + SRA number + clickable SRA badge
Distance-selling pre-contract information (CCRs 2013)
Breach-notification readiness (Saudi PDPL)
Data Protection Officer where required + contact
VAT number displayed where VAT-registered
Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration
Modern Slavery statement (turnover GBP 36M+)
Clear price marking incl. unit/total price

pointers.fact: Consumer Rights Act statutory information
pointers.layman_explanation: Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 you must give consumers clear pre-contract information (trader identity, total price, the main service characteristics, and cancellation rights) before they commit. Missing this makes contracts harder to enforce and draws Trading Standards action.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia publishes a compliant pre-contract information block.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds a compliant pre-contract information block: trader identity, total price, key service terms and the consumer cancellation right.
pointers.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024: direct consumer-law fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

bingo.problem: Right now: Consumer Rights Act statutory information.
bingo.fix: Tamazia publishes a compliant pre-contract information block.

pointers.fact: Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees
pointers.layman_explanation: CMA Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 requires you to show total prices without drip-pricing. The CMA has powers to fine 10% of global turnover.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia deploys a redesigned pricing flow.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia redesigns the pricing flow to show total price upfront and removes drip-pricing patterns.
pointers.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024: direct consumer-law fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

bingo.problem: Right now: Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees.
bingo.fix: Tamazia deploys a redesigned pricing flow.

pointers.fact: Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page
pointers.layman_explanation: Your privacy notice does not name who the legal "data controller" is. Visitors cannot tell who is collecting their data. ICO treats this as a first-mover breach of Article 13(1)(a) and opens an inquiry within weeks.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block (registered name, company number, registered office, ICO registration) and inserts it as paragraph 1 of the privacy notice.
pointers.enforcement_example: ICO v doorstep canvasser 2023: £130,000 for failing to identify controller

bingo.problem: Right now: Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page.
bingo.fix: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block.

pointers.fact: Retention period or criteria
pointers.layman_explanation: You do not state how long personal data is retained. Article 13(2)(a) requires either a specific period or the criteria used to determine it. Indefinite retention is itself a breach.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia writes a retention schedule per data category.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia drafts a retention schedule per data category (e.g. marketing 24 months, accounting 7 years) and adds it to the privacy notice.
pointers.enforcement_example: ICO v Clearview 2022: £7.5m for indefinite retention

bingo.problem: Right now: Retention period or criteria.
bingo.fix: Tamazia writes a retention schedule per data category.

pointers.fact: Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object
pointers.layman_explanation: Visitors cannot find their statutory rights: access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection. Article 13(2)(b) requires you to enumerate them and explain how to exercise each.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia embeds the data-subject-rights section.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia drafts the data-subject-rights section (one paragraph per right) plus the rights request workflow (email + verification flow).

bingo.problem: Right now: Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object.
bingo.fix: Tamazia embeds the data-subject-rights section.

pointers.fact: Right to withdraw consent (if consent is the basis)
pointers.layman_explanation: Where processing is based on consent, Article 13(2)(c) requires you to say how that consent can be withdrawn. Withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia wires a one-click withdrawal route.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds a "How to withdraw your consent" block and wires a one-click withdrawal route into the preference centre.

bingo.problem: Right now: Right to withdraw consent (if consent is the basis).
bingo.fix: Tamazia wires a one-click withdrawal route.

pointers.fact: Solicitors must publish price information for personal injury work.
pointers.layman_explanation: Solicitors must publish price information for personal injury work. This is a specific requirement of the SRA Standards and Regulations, which the SRA enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia configures price information for personal injury work.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Solicitors must publish price information for personal injury work" on your site so it satisfies the SRA Standards and Regulations.
pointers.evidence_quote: And the role of law has never been more pivotal in all of this.
pointers.enforcement_example: SRA: internal fines and referral to the Solicitors Disciplinary Tribunal, which can impose unlimited fines and strike-off; decisions published at sra.org.uk.

bingo.problem: Right now: Solicitors must publish price information for personal injury work. Here it is on your site: “And the role of law has never been more pivotal in all of this.”.
bingo.fix: Tamazia configures price information for personal injury work.

pointers.fact: Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews)
pointers.layman_explanation: The DMCC Act 2024 bans fake and undisclosed-incentivised reviews and the CMA can fine up to 10 percent of global turnover. If you display reviews or testimonials you must be able to show they are genuine and disclose any incentive.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia removes unsubstantiated testimonials.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds review verification and incentive disclosure, and removes any testimonial that cannot be substantiated.
pointers.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024: direct consumer-law fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

bingo.problem: Right now: Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews).
bingo.fix: Tamazia removes unsubstantiated testimonials.

pointers.fact: Whether provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of not providing
pointers.layman_explanation: Where data collection is required by law or by a contract, Article 13(2)(e) requires you to say so and explain the consequences of not providing it.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the statutory-or-contractual-requirement disclosure.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds the statutory-or-contractual-requirement disclosure per form/data-point.

bingo.fix: Tamazia adds the statutory-or-contractual-requirement disclosure.

pointers.fact: Links are not crawlable
pointers.layman_explanation: Search engines may use `href` attributes on links to crawl websites. Ensure that the `href` attribute of anchor elements links to an appropriate destination, so more pages of the site can be discovered. Measured live by Google PageSpeed Insights on your mobile site.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia makes every link crawlable.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia makes every link crawlable so equity and discovery flow.

pointers.fact: For "law firms london" you are not visible in the top 100; lawfuel.com owns position #2.
pointers.layman_explanation: When your buyers search "law firms london", they find lawfuel.com, not you. Page two and below is effectively zero clicks, so this exact query hands its high-intent traffic to a competitor every day, and AI answers cite the top-ranked pages, so being invisible here also keeps you out of the AI results. The firms winning your buyer queries: lawfuel.com, womblebonddickinson.com, farrer.co.uk.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia optimises the page depth and authority.

Those slots go to firms with a complete, consistent local presence. You are missing 1 of those signals, so you are structurally excluded from the results buyers tap first.

pointers.fact: Your Domain Authority is 25/100. Your top 3 competitors: womblebonddickinson.com 40/100, lawfuel.com 30/100, farrer.co.uk 25/100.
pointers.layman_explanation: Domain Authority is the backlink-trust score (here on a 0 to 100 scale) that Google and AI engines use to decide who to rank and cite. womblebonddickinson.com carries 1.6 times of it than you, which is the structural reason these firms sit above you for your buyer queries and get cited in AI answers while you do not. Closing a rank gap without closing the authority gap rarely holds.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the digital-PR and authority-building programme (earned links, citations, entity coverage) that lifts your Domain Authority toward the firms outranking you.

pointers.fact: Legacy JavaScript
pointers.layman_explanation: Polyfills and transforms enable older browsers to use new JavaScript features. However, many aren't necessary for modern browsers. Consider modifying your JavaScript build process to not transpile features, unless you know you must support older browsers. Measured live by Google PageSpeed Insights on your mobile site.

pointers.fact: Forced reflow
pointers.layman_explanation: A forced reflow occurs when JavaScript queries geometric properties (such as offsetWidth) after styles have been invalidated by a change to the DOM state. This can result in poor performance. Learn more about and possible mitigations. Measured live by Google PageSpeed Insights on your mobile site.

pointers.layman_explanation: Answer engines preferentially quote structured Q&A. With no FAQPage you miss the single format LLMs cite most when answering buyer questions.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds an FAQ programme with FAQPage schema targeting real buyer questions.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia builds an FAQ programme with FAQPage schema targeting real buyer questions.

pointers.fact: No Review or AggregateRating schema.

pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds compliant Review/AggregateRating schema (DMCC-safe, genuine reviews only).
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds compliant Review/AggregateRating schema (DMCC-safe, genuine reviews only).

pointers.fact: No BreadcrumbList schema.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds BreadcrumbList schema sitewide.

bingo.problem: Right now: No BreadcrumbList schema.
bingo.fix: Tamazia adds BreadcrumbList schema sitewide.

pointers.fact: No bylined author or Person schema establishing expertise.
pointers.layman_explanation: Google E-E-A-T and LLM trust models reward demonstrable Experience, Expertise, Authority and Trust. Anonymous content is discounted and rarely cited in AI answers.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships a bylined expert-author programme with Person schema, credentials and sameAs.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia ships a bylined expert-author programme with Person schema, credentials and sameAs.

pointers.fact: For "law firms london London", the authoritative sources AI and Google read are legal500.com, chambers.com and your site is not among the top results.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answers and Google both lean on a handful of trusted sources for each category (directories, review sites, trade press, Wikipedia). For your buyer query those sources are legal500.com, chambers.com. Your competitors are listed and cited there; you are not, so the engines repeat what those sources say and leave you out.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia gets you placed, complete and well-reviewed on the exact authority sources that feed AI and Google for your category.

pointers.fact: Your domain is not signed with DNSSEC.
pointers.layman_explanation: Without DNSSEC, attackers can forge DNS responses (cache poisoning) to silently redirect your visitors or intercept email. Enterprise, finance and government client security reviews increasingly require it.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.

needs_review.fact: Must publish a policy declaring no incentivised, fake or undisclosed reviews.
needs_review.layman_explanation: From 2025, hosting fake or incentivised reviews without disclosure is a CMA fineable offence. The fine reaches £300k or 10% turnover. Buyers and platforms are both in scope.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts a Reviews Policy page meeting CMA wording, runs a review-audit on the live site, and updates the review-collection flow to meet disclosure standards.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia drafts a Reviews Policy page meeting CMA wording, runs a review-audit on the live site, and updates the review-collection flow to meet disclosure standards.
needs_review.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024 (in force 6 April 2025): direct fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

needs_review.fact: Reviews or testimonials shown without disclosing incentives or a genuine-review verification process
needs_review.layman_explanation: If you display reviews or testimonials, you must not feature fake ones and must disclose any that were incentivised or gifted. The CMA can fine up to 10 percent of worldwide turnover.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Add review-verification and an incentive-disclosure process, and remove any testimonials you cannot substantiate.
needs_review.recommendation: Add review-verification and an incentive-disclosure process, and remove any testimonials you cannot substantiate.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The DMCC Act fake-review ban came into force on 6 April 2025; the CMA began enforcement from July 2025 and can fine up to 10 percent of global annual turnover without going to court. Businesses have a positive duty to prevent and remove fake and undisclosed-incentivised reviews.

needs_review.fact: Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
needs_review.layman_explanation: Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All. This is a specific requirement of UK PECR, which the ICO enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and func" on your site so it satisfies UK PECR.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and func" on your site so it satisfies UK PECR.
needs_review.enforcement_example: DUAA 2025 raised the maximum PECR fine from 500000 to 17.5M or 4 percent of global turnover. The ICO is sweeping the top 1000 UK sites; in its first batch of 200, 134 firms were warned and given 30 days to fix non-compliant banners.

needs_review.fact: Complaints procedure + Legal Ombudsman + SRA routes
needs_review.layman_explanation: The SRA requires a published complaints procedure naming the Legal Ombudsman and SRA routes. Yours is missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the SRA-compliant complaints procedure.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds the SRA-compliant complaints procedure.
needs_review.enforcement_example: SRA Standards require a published complaints procedure with Legal Ombudsman and SRA escalation routes.

needs_review.citation: UK PECR + UK GDPR · cookie consent
needs_review.fact: 6 non-essential tracker(s) load with no detectable consent mechanism: Google Analytics (sets _ga, _gali, _ga_ — Analytics, controller Google); Google Tag Manager (sets td — Marketing, controller Google); Google Ads / DoubleClick (sets Conversion, _gcl_aw, _gcl_gs — Advertising, controller Google); LinkedIn Insight Tag (sets lidc, bcookie, bscookie — Advertising, controller LinkedIn); HubSpot (sets __hstc, hubspotutk, __hssc — Analytics, controller HubSpot); Microsoft Advertising (UET) (sets MR, MUID, MUIDB — Advertising, controller Microsoft).
needs_review.layman_explanation: These trackers set cookies and share data with Google, LinkedIn, HubSpot, Microsoft the moment the page loads, before the visitor consents. That breaches UK PECR + UK GDPR. You serve clients in UK, Middle East, Global, so each of those regimes applies regardless of where the firm is registered. The ICO is actively reviewing the UK's top 1,000 sites and the maximum PECR fine is now £17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia installs a consent platform that blocks every non-essential tracker until opt-in, with a Reject-All button as prominent as Accept-All.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia installs a consent platform that blocks every non-essential tracker until opt-in, with a Reject-All button as prominent as Accept-All.
needs_review.evidence: homepage HTML · 6 non-essential trackers, no consent gate · controllers: Google, LinkedIn, HubSpot, Microsoft
needs_review.enforcement_example: ICO under PECR: enforcement notices and fines up to GBP 500,000, plus GDPR-level fines where consent is the lawful basis; the ICO has run cookie-consent sweeps against major UK sites.

needs_review.layman_explanation: Saudi PDPL restricts and requires disclosure of transfers outside the Kingdom; your notice is silent.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia documents Saudi cross-border transfer safeguards.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia documents Saudi cross-border transfer safeguards.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Saudi PDPL restricts and requires disclosure of transfers outside the Kingdom.

needs_review.fact: Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
needs_review.layman_explanation: Selling online to UK consumers means you must tell them about their 14-day right to cancel and your returns and refund policy before they buy.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
needs_review.recommendation: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 give distance-selling consumers a 14-day cancellation right that must be communicated; the Consumer Rights Act 2015 governs refunds. Trading Standards enforce and the DMCC raises consumer-law fines to 10 percent of turnover.

needs_review.layman_explanation: If children are likely to use your service, the ICO Children Code requires age-appropriate design; not evident.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia applies the ICO Children Code standards.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia applies the ICO Children Code standards.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The ICO Children Code requires age-appropriate design for services likely accessed by children.

needs_review.layman_explanation: UK residents can request erasure; the route is not explained.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 17; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.fact: UK right to object to direct marketing
needs_review.layman_explanation: UK residents can object to direct marketing; not disclosed.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the objection right and opt-out.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds the objection right and opt-out.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 21; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 28 processor transparency is not addressed.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 28; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 30 ROPA expected; none referenced.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 30; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.fact: Operates across 3 regions (UK, Middle East, Global) on one-size-fits-all compliance.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Operating across UK, Middle East, Global means several data-protection and advertising regimes apply at once. A single generic policy rarely satisfies all of them, which is the most common compliance gap for international firms and a direct credibility risk in regulated sectors.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia maps each market to its regime and closes the gaps.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia maps each market to its regime and closes the gaps.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Enforced by the framework regulator through penalties and sanctions per the statute (see citation).

needs_review.layman_explanation: Saudi PDPL requires breach notification to SDAIA; no posture is evident.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia documents a Saudi breach-notification process.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia documents a Saudi breach-notification process.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Saudi PDPL requires breach notification to SDAIA and affected data subjects.

exec_summary: The most serious regulatory risk is the potential GBP 61,750,000 fine exposure due to multiple P0 findings, including non-compliance with the Consumer Rights Act and inadequate disclosure of pricing transparency, identity of the controller, and data subject rights, which matters as it poses a significant threat to the company's financial stability and reputation. Fixing these critical issues presents an opportunity to enhance transparency, build trust with clients, and demonstrate commitment to regulatory compliance, thereby protecting the brand and unlocking potential growth in the legal serv

news_map.UK_CMA: CMA (2026-05-28): Court endorses CMA action as Emma Sleep agrees to change sales practices. Source: https://www.gov.uk/government/news/court-endorses-cma-action-as-emma-sleep-agrees-to-change-sales-practices
news_map.UK_GDPR_A13: ICO issued GBP 19.6M across 7 cases in 2025 (Capita GBP 14M, Advanced Computer Software GBP 3.07M, 23andMe GBP 2.31M, LastPass GBP 1.23M); two-thirds were UK GDPR breaches.
news_map.UK_DPA_2018: ICO 2025 enforcement hit GBP 19.6M from 7 cases (vs GBP 2.7M in 2024); the DUAA came into force 5 Feb 2026 with new compulsion powers.
news_map.UK_PECR: The DUAA came into force 5 Feb 2026, raising the maximum PECR fine to GBP 17.5M (from GBP 500k); the ICO is reviewing the UK top 1,000 websites cookie banners.
news_map.UK_ICO_COOKIES: DUAA (in force 5 Feb 2026) lifts PECR fines to GBP 17.5M; the ICO is actively reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites and warned non-compliant cookie banners.
news_map.UK_SRA_COC: SRA 2025 warning notice on no-win-no-fee marketing. SRA Transparency Rules sweeps run quarterly.
news_map.GOOGLE_EEAT: Google March 2024 core update emphasised E-E-A-T; sites without author bylines saw 31% traffic drop.
news_map.UK_DMCC_2024: CMA gained direct fining powers up to 10% global turnover from April 2025.
news_map.UK_COMPANIES_ACT: Companies House active enforcement of website disclosure post Economic Crime Act 2023.
news_map.UK_EQUALITY_2010: EHRC 2024 digital-accessibility code. Damages claims up 18% in 2024.
news_map.UK_CRA_2015: CMA confirms CRA 2015 applies in parallel with DMCC. Cross-referenced in 2025 enforcement.

signals.title: Mishcon de Reya. It's business. But it's personal.

audits.title: Improve image delivery
audits.description: Reducing the download time of images can improve the perceived load time of the page and LCP.

audits.title: Legacy JavaScript
audits.description: Polyfills and transforms enable older browsers to use new JavaScript features. However, many aren't necessary for modern browsers. Consider modifying your JavaScript build process to not transpile features, unless you know you must support older browsers.

audits.title: Links are not crawlable
audits.description: Search engines may use `href` attributes on links to crawl websites. Ensure that the `href` attribute of anchor elements links to an appropriate destination, so more pages of the site can be discovered.
audits.node_snippet: <a class="btn btn-primary" data-bs-target="#modalPopup" data-bs-toggle="modal" data-mediaid="1088134932" data-template="vimeo" data-title="" data-toggle="modal" type="button">

audits.title: First Contentful Paint
audits.description: First Contentful Paint marks the time at which the first text or image is painted..

audits.title: Time to Interactive
audits.description: Time to Interactive is the amount of time it takes for the page to become fully interactive..

audits.title: Reduce unused CSS
audits.description: Reduce unused rules from stylesheets and defer CSS not used for above-the-fold content to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

audits.title: Speed Index
audits.description: Speed Index shows how quickly the contents of a page are visibly populated..

audits.title: Network dependency tree
audits.description: by reducing the length of chains, reducing the download size of resources, or deferring the download of unnecessary resources to improve page load.

audits.title: Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio.
audits.description: Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read..

audits.title: Avoid multiple page redirects
audits.description: Redirects introduce additional delays before the page can be loaded..

audits.title: Links do not have descriptive text
audits.description: Descriptive link text helps search engines understand your content..

audits.title: Render-blocking requests
audits.description: Requests are blocking the page's initial render, which may delay LCP. can move these network requests out of the critical path.

audits.title: Forced reflow
audits.description: A forced reflow occurs when JavaScript queries geometric properties (such as offsetWidth) after styles have been invalidated by a change to the DOM state. This can result in poor performance. Learn more about and possible mitigations.

audits.title: Document request latency
audits.description: Your first network request is the most important. by avoiding redirects, ensuring a fast server response, and enabling text compression.

audits.title: Largest Contentful Paint
audits.description: Largest Contentful Paint marks the time at which the largest text or image is painted.

regimes.why: applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: mishcon is registered in the United Kingdom and its own website shows it serves clients in the UK, the Middle East and globally. It is therefore bound by UK law (UK GDPR, PECR, CMA/DMCC, sector regulators), Gulf law (UAE PDPL, DIFC/ADGM, RERA where applicable) and multiple international regimes, not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.

terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
